findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Re-confirm run_differs.py's actual backend-load line number and probe-dispatch call position in the post-4af8-landed codebase before implementing aff0's hoist, and update the approach description with correct coordinates.
evidence: aff0 Approach: 'today the backend is loaded at run_differs.py:234 — AFTER the probe dispatch call at :230 — so the _load_reconcile_backend() call (run_differs.py:62-75...) is hoisted above the probe dispatch'
Parent decomposition: '4af8-2d88-719e-4478 — vendor mapper entrypoints injected into outbound_differ/inbound_differ/apply_inbound via the port (914)' — marked as landed/closed

4af8 Delivered: 'run_differs: production injection point — obtains the configured Backend once and threads backend.outbound / backend.inbound into both differ phases. Injected params default to None, resolving via the neutral select_backend seam'
4af8 scope includes run_differs.py at :481-495 for outbound_mapper injection — the backend object is already in scope in the post-4af8 file, potentially already above :230
findings.finding: aff0's approach description references stale run_differs.py line numbers (:230, :234, :369) that reflect the pre-4af8 state of the file. 4af8 is already landed and has modified run_differs.py to inject the backend object at the differ call sites (:481-495). The hoist logic ('today the backend is loaded at run_differs.py:234 — AFTER the probe dispatch call at :230') describes a file state that no longer exists, leaving the implementer without accurate coordinates for the required edit.
findings.location: child aff0-b0d9-e11c-4b6a — Approach section, run_differs.py paragraph
scenarios: Implementer of aff0 reads :234 as the backend load site and discovers the line no longer exists or refers to something different after 4af8's edits, requiring re-investigation of the correct hoist point.

If 4af8 already hoists the backend load above :230 as a side-effect of threading the backend into the differs (which are called later in the function), the hoist in aff0 may already be done — or it may still be needed but at a different line.
findings.suggested_fix: Update aff0's Approach section to say 'after 4af8 lands, re-grep run_differs.py for the current backend-load site and probe-dispatch call; hoist the backend resolution if and only if the probe dispatch still precedes the backend assignment in the post-4af8 function body; update line-number references accordingly.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Serialize 625b and 21ca so that the child landing second explicitly bases its inbound_fields.py edit on the post-first-landing state, or declare 21ca depends_on 625b (or vice versa) and document inbound_fields.py as a shared file with the same additive exemption the parent grants to _backend.py/adapters/jira/backend.py.
evidence: 625b Scope: 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_fields.py (Jira mapper: aliases, parent remote id, reporter aliases)'
21ca Scope: 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_fields.py (Jira mapper: normalize_rich_text implementation)'

Parent success criteria: 'children do not overlap (each owns disjoint core modules; shared _backend.py/adapters/jira/backend.py edits are additive per child and recorded in each child's file impact)' — inbound_fields.py is not _backend.py or adapters/jira/backend.py, so this exemption does not apply
findings.finding: 625b and 21ca are both open children that list inbound_fields.py in scope, violating the parent's 'disjoint edit-sets' invariant. 625b adds three new canonical key outputs to the Jira InboundMapper's map_remote_to_local (assignee_identity, remote_parent_id, reporter_identity); 21ca adds normalize_rich_text to the same file's InboundMapper implementation. If developed and reviewed in parallel these stories will produce merge conflicts in inbound_fields.py.
scenarios: Both stories are picked up concurrently; both implementers edit inbound_fields.py; the merge produces a conflict requiring a coordinated resolution not planned in either ticket.
21ca lands first and adds normalize_rich_text; 625b lands after and its patch still targets the pre-21ca line numbers, requiring re-diffing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a serialization constraint between 625b and aff0 (e.g., aff0 depends_on 625b or vice versa) or document run_differs.py as a shared additive file for these two stories with explicit conflict-resolution instructions.
evidence: 625b Scope: 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/run_differs.py (pass inbound_mapper=backend.inbound)'
aff0 Scope: 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/run_differs.py (capability-guarded routing at the probe call site)'
Parent: 'Open... independently reviewable, parallel-safe' — but parallel-safety requires disjoint edit-sets
findings.finding: 625b and aff0 are both open children that list run_differs.py in scope, again violating the 'disjoint edit-sets' claim for a file not in the parent's shared-file exemption list. 625b adds 'inbound_mapper=backend.inbound' to compute_outbound_mutations (run_differs.py:481-495 region); aff0 hoists the backend load above the probe dispatch call. If developed in parallel these produce merge conflicts.
scenarios: 625b and aff0 are reviewed and merged simultaneously; both produce run_differs.py edits; merge conflict requires coordinated resolution not described in either ticket.
aff0 lands first with its hoist; 625b's patch to run_differs.py no longer applies cleanly because line numbers have shifted.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update 625b's Scope entry for _backend.py to read '(OutboundMapper Protocol: map_fields_to_remote, resolve_assignee; InboundMapper contract note: canonical output incl. identity aliases + parent remote id)' and add an AC item verifying the OutboundMapper extension is present in _backend.py and implemented by JiraBackend.
evidence: 625b Scope: 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_backend.py (InboundMapper contract note: canonical output incl. identity aliases + parent remote id)' — mentions only InboundMapper
625b body step 4b: 'OutboundMapper gains resolve_assignee(local_value: str, remote_identity: dict | None) -> tuple[Any, bool, bool]' — this is a Protocol method addition, not merely a 'contract note'
625b body step 3: 'a NEW OutboundMapper method map_fields_to_remote(local_fields: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]' — another Protocol method addition
625b AC does not include a criterion verifying that the OutboundMapper Protocol is extended with these two methods and that JiraBackend implements them
findings.finding: 625b's Scope entry for _backend.py understates the Protocol changes it makes: it reads '(InboundMapper contract note: canonical output incl. identity aliases + parent remote id)' but the story body also adds two new methods to the OutboundMapper Protocol (resolve_assignee and map_fields_to_remote) — a material Protocol extension that affects every OutboundMapper implementor and the contract suite. The scope line omission means reviewers checking the scope against the body will find an inconsistency, and the AC does not explicitly verify the OutboundMapper extension.
findings.location: child 625b-9cd1-1ce9-4e5f — Scope (_backend.py line) and Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: A reviewer approves 625b based on its scope line (InboundMapper only) and misses that OutboundMapper is also extended, leaving the OutboundMapper extension unreviewed.

The implementer follows the scope line and only adds the InboundMapper canonical keys, missing the OutboundMapper Protocol extension — map_fields_to_remote and resolve_assignee are not defined on the Protocol, causing the outbound differ's emission boundary to fail at typecheck.
findings.suggested_fix: Amend 625b Scope: replace '(InboundMapper contract note: canonical output incl. identity aliases + parent remote id)' with '(OutboundMapper Protocol: new map_fields_to_remote + resolve_assignee methods; InboundMapper contract note: canonical output incl. identity aliases, parent remote id, reporter aliases)'. Add AC: '[ ] OutboundMapper Protocol in _backend.py defines map_fields_to_remote and resolve_assignee; JiraBackend's outbound delegate implements both — verified by make typecheck green and a contract-suite case exercising each method via FakeBackend.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit AC items to 625b and 21ca requiring that FakeBackend is updated to implement all new Protocol members introduced by each story, mirroring 97f2's 'Update the S2 contract suite and the test-only FakeBackend in the same change' requirement.
evidence: 625b adds to OutboundMapper Protocol: 'OutboundMapper gains map_fields_to_remote(local_fields: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]' and 'OutboundMapper gains resolve_assignee(local_value: str, remote_identity: dict | None) -> tuple[Any, bool, bool]' — neither the 625b Scope nor its AC mentions updating FakeBackend
21ca adds to InboundMapper Protocol: 'InboundMapper gains normalize_rich_text(body: Any) -> str' and FieldSanitizer gains comment-fit surface — the 21ca Scope does not list FakeBackend update

ce4d establishes FakeBackend as the in-tree test double exercising all Protocol members: 'a FakeBackend (test-only; lives under tests/, never shipped)' — widening the Protocol without updating FakeBackend causes the contract suite parameterized over it to fail

97f2 correctly states: 'Update the S2 contract suite and the test-only FakeBackend in the same change so every in-tree Backend implementor satisfies the widened Protocol' — this precedent is not replicated in 625b or 21ca
findings.finding: Neither 625b nor 21ca explicitly commits to updating the test-only FakeBackend when they widen the Backend Protocol. 625b adds OutboundMapper.resolve_assignee and map_fields_to_remote to the OutboundMapper Protocol; 21ca adds normalize_rich_text to InboundMapper and a comment-fit method to FieldSanitizer. FakeBackend (defined in the contract suite from ce4d, used by test_backend_contract.py and the neutrality gate) must implement all Protocol members to satisfy structural typing and the contract suite; without explicit planned updates it will fail typecheck and the contract suite after each story lands.
findings.location: child 625b-9cd1-1ce9-4e5f — Scope and Acceptance Criteria; child 21ca-cdb4-671c-460c — Scope and Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: 625b lands; FakeBackend lacks resolve_assignee and map_fields_to_remote; mypy/pyright fails on the FakeBackend type annotation; 'make typecheck' is red.
21ca lands; FakeBackend lacks normalize_rich_text; the contract suite's InboundMapper round-trip test fails for FakeBackend; 'make test' is red.
Both stories land and FakeBackend is updated ad hoc only in one, leaving the other's new Protocol members unimplemented — typecheck and test failures appear only when both are merged.
findings.suggested_fix: In 625b, add to Scope: 'tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_contract.py (FakeBackend: implement resolve_assignee and map_fields_to_remote on its OutboundMapper delegate)'. In 21ca, add to Scope: 'tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_contract.py (FakeBackend: implement normalize_rich_text on InboundMapper delegate; implement comment-fit on FieldSanitizer delegate)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign a ticket ID to each of the four unnamed open children ('Canonical-shape outbound field diff', 'Canonical link diff', 'Vendor absence probe', 'Rich-text codec + comment limits') so the Success Criteria's 'every deliverable is covered by exactly one child' is verifiable without a clarifying question.
evidence: 'Every deliverable above is covered by exactly one child' (Success Criteria)

97f2 and ad44 have explicit ticket IDs; 'Canonical-shape outbound field diff', 'Canonical link diff', 'Vendor absence probe behind SupportsAbsenceProbe', 'Rich-text codec + comment limits behind the port' have none
findings.finding: Four open child work items ('Canonical-shape outbound field diff', 'Canonical link diff', 'Vendor absence probe behind SupportsAbsenceProbe', 'Rich-text codec + comment limits behind the port') are described in the 'Open' decomposition section but have no ticket IDs. The Success Criteria assert 'every deliverable above is covered by exactly one child', yet an executing agent cannot verify or enforce that invariant for the four unnamed items — it cannot tell whether tickets already exist, need to be created, or are being folded into 97f2/ad44.
findings.location: Child decomposition — Open section; Success Criteria paragraph
scenarios: An agent begins work on 'Canonical link diff' and cannot reference or close a ticket, cannot confirm it isn't already tracked elsewhere, and cannot satisfy the 'covered by exactly one child' invariant.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a ticket stub line (format matching existing entries, e.g. `- `<id>` — Canonical-shape outbound field diff — …`) for each of the four unnamed items, or note explicitly that ticket creation is the first action of whoever picks up each item.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign ticket IDs to the three ticket-less open children ('Canonical-shape outbound field diff', 'Vendor absence probe behind `SupportsAbsenceProbe`', 'Rich-text codec + comment limits behind the port') so the Success Criteria's 'covered by exactly one child' claim holds for all deliverables.
evidence: 'Canonical-shape outbound field diff' — no ticket ID assigned
'Vendor absence probe behind `SupportsAbsenceProbe`' — no ticket ID assigned
'Rich-text codec + comment limits behind the port' — no ticket ID assigned
Success Criteria: 'every deliverable above is covered by exactly one child; children do not overlap'
findings.finding: Three open children — 'Canonical-shape outbound field diff', 'Vendor absence probe behind `SupportsAbsenceProbe`', and 'Rich-text codec + comment limits behind the port' — are fully described deliverables but carry no ticket IDs. The Success Criteria states 'every deliverable above is covered by exactly one child; children do not overlap', which is unverifiable for these three because they are not formally constituted as children.
findings.location: Child decomposition — Open children section; Success Criteria paragraph
scenarios: An implementer cannot reference these work items in dependency chains, commit messages, or tracking systems, making the 'covered by exactly one child' guarantee hollow for three of the six open deliverables.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (e.g., a temporary re-export shim window with a removal commit, or an expand-contract sequence) for each open child that physically relocates modules, so a missed import site has a defined recovery path rather than an immediate hard break.
evidence: '8 vendor modules physically relocated into adapters/jira/ (acli*, adf, outbound_fields, comment_limits) + loader/import updates (905)'
'no re-export shims' policy stated explicitly for S5
Open children follow same relocation pattern with no rollback language anywhere in the plan
T4 rubric: 'The remedy for a destructive/breaking change is an explicit ROLLBACK / back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing'
findings.finding: The plan explicitly prohibits re-export shims for all relocated modules (S5 landed; same pattern in open children for rich-text codec, comment limits, canonical diff modules) but states no rollback or back-out plan. If a consumer import site is missed, there is no defined revert mechanism — the plan acknowledges the break-by-policy but does not supply the required reversibility mechanism (expand-contract sequencing or explicit back-out steps).
findings.location: Child decomposition — S5 description ('no re-export shims'); open children 'Rich-text codec + comment limits behind the port'; AC bullet S4/S5
scenarios: A consumer import site (test file, downstream tool, or CI script) references an old package-root path and is not caught by the neutrality sweep; with no shim and no rollback plan, the breakage is discovered only at runtime or in a downstream repo with no defined recovery path.
An open child relocates additional modules; a missed import causes a red build on main with no defined revert strategy documented.
findings.suggested_fix: For each open relocation child, add a two-step expand-contract: Step 1 — add the module at the new path and add a re-export shim at the old path with a deprecation warning; Step 2 (follow-up commit after confirming all consumers updated) — delete the shim. This makes the migration reversible at each step. Alternatively, document an explicit 'revert commit' strategy (e.g., 'if main goes red, revert change <X> as a unit') in each child's rollback section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add (or reference) a config-reference doc update for the `config.reconciler.backend` key — covering key name, valid values, default (`"jira"`), and effect — either as an ADR 0035 §(d) subsection or an entry in the project's configuration reference.
evidence: 'S3 94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75 — reconciler.backend config + in-tree registry + select_backend() (902)' — no doc update mentioned
'default "jira" reproduces prior behavior end-to-end' — implies operators could set non-default values, requiring documentation
T7 rubric: 'a new pattern/contract/config/CLI gets a doc/ADR'
ADR 0035 §(d) amendment described covers architectural interface decisions but config-key semantics are not mentioned as part of the ADR update
findings.finding: The new `config.reconciler.backend` configuration key (S3, landed as change 902) is a new operator-visible config surface, but the plan contains no mention of updating any configuration reference documentation, README, or operator guide to document the new key, its valid values, and its default.
findings.location: Child decomposition — S3 description; AC bullet 'Backend chosen via config.reconciler.backend through the registry'
scenarios: An operator or integrator attempts to configure a non-default backend and has no documented reference for the key name, allowed values, or default — they must read source code.
A future ADR or runbook references the config schema and finds the new key undocumented, causing confusion about when it was introduced and what it controls.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a documentation sweep step (or explicit 'no public doc references these paths' assertion) for each physical module relocation (S5 and open children) to confirm no developer-facing docs, READMEs, or architecture diagrams reference the old package-root paths.
evidence: 'acli*, adf, outbound_fields, comment_limits' relocated from package-root to adapters/jira/ — no doc-sweep step mentioned
T7 rubric: 'The change doesn't strand stale references (deleted/renamed artifacts still referenced)'
ABSENCE: no 'update docs/references' step in S5 description or any open child's description
findings.finding: The plan relocates 8 vendor modules (S5, change 905) with 'no re-export shims' and states only that 'loader/import updates' were made, but does not mention a sweep or update of any developer-facing documentation (module-level docstrings, internal wiki pages, architecture diagrams, or README import examples) that may reference the old package-root paths for those modules (`acli*`, `adf`, `outbound_fields`, `comment_limits`). Stale path references in docs would strand readers.
findings.location: Child decomposition — S5 ('8 vendor modules physically relocated into adapters/jira/'); open children for rich-text codec relocation
scenarios: A developer follows a README or architecture doc that says 'see rebar_reconciler.adf for ADF utilities' and finds the module gone from that path, with no pointer to the new location.
An internal onboarding doc references old import paths; a new team member wastes time tracing the rename.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in S3's design (or the Success Criteria) whether `select_backend()` supports zero-core-edit extension (e.g., via entry-points, a plugin dict keyed by convention, or a documented `register_backend()` call in `adapters/<x>/__init__.py`), and if a core edit is required for each new backend, explicitly acknowledge and justify that coupling in ADR 0035 or the registry's docstring.
evidence: 'A second backend can be introduced by implementing `adapters/<x>/` against the Backend port alone: no package-root core module names, imports, lazy-loads, or shape-compares anything Jira-specific.'
S3 description: 'reconciler.backend config + in-tree registry + select_backend()' — 'in-tree registry' implies a code-side lookup table, not a plugin-discovery mechanism
ABSENCE: no plan text describes how the registry resolves a new key (e.g., `"github_issues"`) without a core edit
findings.finding: The in-tree registry introduced by S3 (`select_backend()`) does not specify whether adding a second backend requires a code change to the registry itself or whether backends self-register (e.g., via entry-points, a plugin dict, or a discovery convention). If a core file must be edited for each new vendor, the stated goal — 'a second backend can be introduced by implementing `adapters/<x>/` against the Backend port alone: no package-root core module names' — is partially undermined, and that coupling should be acknowledged and mitigated in the registry's design.
findings.location: Child decomposition — S3 (`94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75`); Success Criteria paragraph
scenarios: An engineer implements `adapters/github_issues/` against the Backend port but must also edit a core `_registry.py` mapping — violating the stated 'no core rewrite' guarantee and creating the coupling the seam was designed to prevent.
The registry lookup raises `KeyError` for an unregistered backend at runtime with no guidance on how to extend it, discouraging adoption of the seam.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-sentence clarification to the Success Criteria: e.g., 'The registry resolves backend keys via [entry-points / a `BACKENDS` dict in `adapters/__init__.py` / explicit `register_backend()` calls]; adding `adapters/github_issues/` requires [no core edit / exactly one line in the registry dict].' If a core edit is unavoidable, record it as a known coupling in ADR 0035 §(d) alongside the extension procedure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the canonical-shape outbound field diff child, specify how assignee comparison is handled post-canonicalization: either (a) document that `InboundMapper` produces a stable canonical form that covers all local identity forms (and update the contract suite to assert this), or (b) retain `_assignee_matches` as a canonical (non-Jira-specific) core helper that compares any multi-form remote identity dict against a local string — and record this design choice in the child plan.
evidence: Plan states: 'vendor comparison helpers become adapter-internal' — but `_assignee_matches` encodes Jira-specific identity matching (accountId/displayName/emailAddress dict) that is essential for convergence
`outbound_differ.py:39-44`: `from rebar_reconciler.adapters.jira.outbound_fields import _assignee_matches` — this is the vendor import the canonical-diff child must eliminate

`outbound_fields.py:237-261`: `_assignee_matches` inspects `jira_raw.get('emailAddress')`, `jira_raw.get('accountId')`, `jira_raw.get('displayName')` — it operates on raw Jira shapes, not a canonical form

`inbound_differ.py:187`: even after the InboundMapper injection at line 159, the inbound differ still calls `_assignee_matches(local_assignee, jira_fields.get('assignee'))` against the raw shape — showing canonicalization alone does not resolve the assignee comparison problem
ABSENCE: the plan's canonical-diff child does not mention assignee comparison semantics, `_assignee_matches` handling, or what the canonical InboundMapper assignee output is
findings.finding: The canonical outbound field diff open child's mechanism does not specify how assignee comparison is handled after canonicalization. Currently `_diff_fields` compares local assignee strings against raw Jira `{accountId, displayName, emailAddress}` dicts using `_assignee_matches` (the bug 85a1 fix for phantom-churn). After `InboundMapper.map_remote_to_local` canonicalizes the snapshot, the assignee becomes a scalar (e.g. `displayName`). If the canonical differ then compares local-string vs canonical-string without shape-tolerant matching, it risks re-introducing the multi-form identity phantom-churn regression. The plan does not state what `InboundMapper` produces for `assignee`, whether it is stable across identity forms, or how the canonical comparison avoids regressing bug 85a1.
findings.location: Plan section 'Child decomposition' > 'Canonical-shape outbound field diff' open child; codebase: `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/jira/outbound_fields.py:237-261` (`_assignee_matches`) and `outbound_differ.py:39-44` (direct import of `_assignee_matches` from vendor adapter)
scenarios: A local ticket stores assignee as an email address; InboundMapper canonicalizes the Jira snapshot to displayName; the canonical outbound differ compares email != displayName and emits a spurious update every pass — re-introducing the bug 85a1 idempotency churn
A local ticket stores assignee as an accountId (identity fast-path, ticket 264f); after canonicalization the snapshot holds displayName; comparison fails every pass
findings.suggested_fix: In the canonical-diff child plan, add a note: `InboundMapper.map_remote_to_local` for the Jira backend must map `assignee` to a form that is stable across all local identity representations (email / displayName / accountId). If this is not guaranteed, extract `_assignee_matches` into a core helper (`_canonical_assignee_matches(local: str, canonical_assignee: str) -> bool`) that operates on canonical (already-mapped) strings, and move the multi-form matching into the Jira `InboundMapper` implementation so the canonical form is always the right one for comparison.

evidence: Session log child list includes: '625b-9cd1-1ce9-4e5f: Canonical-shape outbound field diff: inbound-map the snapshot, diff local-vs-local', 'eefd-49b1-995f-488d: Canonical link diff: SupportsLinks translation members replace vendor link-shape comparisons', 'aff0-b0d9-e11c-4b6a: Vendor absence probe behind a SupportsAbsenceProbe capability (relocate Jira REST probe mechanics)', '21ca-cdb4-671c-460c: Rich-text codec and comment limits behind the port: delete the adapters.jira lazy-key literals'
Plan open items describe the same four work areas but carry NO ticket ID, unlike the landed children (S1–S5) and the ad44 open child which all carry IDs.
Plan Success Criteria states 'Every deliverable above is covered by exactly one child' — without IDs the coverage claim cannot be verified against the tracking system.
findings.finding: Four open child tickets from the session log — 625b-9cd1-1ce9-4e5f (canonical outbound field diff), eefd-49b1-995f-488d (canonical link diff), aff0-b0d9-e11c-4b6a (vendor absence probe), and 21ca-cdb4-671c-460c (rich-text codec / comment limits) — appear in the plan's child decomposition as described work items but WITHOUT their ticket IDs, silently dropping the tracking binding that the session log established for each as a discrete child ticket.
scenarios: A reviewer auditing the epic's ticket graph cannot confirm the four open deliverables are assigned to tracked children without manual cross-referencing outside the plan.
If a child ticket is re-scoped or split (as happened to 97f2), the plan's open list gives no anchor to detect the divergence.
The 'blocks -> be74' claim relies on all children being landed; an untracked open child could be silently skipped, allowing the block to be prematurely lifted.

findings.finding: The epic's AC4 records only one exemption to the 'zero vendor reach-through' rule (inbound_fields.py), but reconcile_check.py contains a second, intentional vendor reach-through: it function-nested lazy-loads adapters/jira/outbound_fields.py and calls _extract_jira_field/_assignee_matches for field-level comparison. The neutrality gate test explicitly documents this as deliberate and out of scope (test_backend_neutrality.py:94-99). The AC as written ('every package-root *.py module has NO adapters.jira/acli_subprocess import ... single recorded exemption: inbound_fields.py') and the success criterion ('no package-root core module names, imports, lazy-loads, or shape-compares anything Jira-specific') are both falsified by reconcile_check.py in the reconcile-check mode execution path.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 ('Zero vendor reach-through in core'); Success Criteria ('no package-root core module names, imports, lazy-loads, or shape-compares anything Jira-specific')
findings.suggested_fix: In AC4, change 'single recorded exemption: inbound_fields.py' to 'two recorded exemptions: inbound_fields.py (Jira InboundMapper implementation pending relocation, ticket 556a) and reconcile_check.py (uses vendor field-extraction helpers via lazy by-path load for comparison parity; routed through Backend port by follow-up ticket <new-id>).'

findings.finding: reconcile_check.py's Jira-specific lazy `_load_sibling` import is explicitly documented as a residual in 4af8 ('not this ticket'), but NO sibling in the complete roster covers it, and parent AC4 records only ONE exemption (inbound_fields.py) with no carve-out for reconcile_check. This is a partial-without-remainder gap: 4af8 acknowledges the remainder but neither names the ticket that owns it nor claims it is exempt.
findings.location: child 4af8-2d88-719e-4478, Scope boundary section; parent AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Add reconcile_check.py to the scope of 97f2 (which already rewires scalar Jira imports in core) or create a micro-child for it; update the parent AC4 exemption list to either include it or confirm it will be resolved.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC bullet (or extend AC bullet 3/4) that explicitly covers the `4af8` deliverable: mapper entrypoints injected into outbound_differ/inbound_differ/apply_inbound via the port (change 914), with a stated verification method.
evidence: '`4af8-2d88-719e-4478` — vendor mapper entrypoints injected into outbound_differ/inbound_differ/apply_inbound via the port (914)'

AC bullet 3: 'All transport construction and provider literals route through the port (S4); the 8 vendor modules physically reside under adapters/jira/ with loaders repointed and no re-export shims (S5)' — no mention of 4af8
AC bullet 4: 'verified by the canonical-diff children's characterization and neutrality tests' — scoped to open children, not 4af8's already-landed change
findings.finding: Landed child `4af8-2d88-719e-4478` (vendor mapper entrypoints injected into outbound_differ/inbound_differ/apply_inbound via the port, change 914) is listed as 'Landed and closed' but no AC bullet explicitly names or verifies it. AC bullet 3 covers S4/S5 only; AC bullet 4 references 'canonical-diff children's characterization and neutrality tests' (open work). The injection of mapper entrypoints — the mechanism that decouples outbound_differ/inbound_differ from Jira — is an orphan deliverable with no acceptance criterion.
findings.location: Child decomposition — `4af8-2d88-719e-4478` entry; Acceptance Criteria bullets 3 and 4
scenarios: An auditor checking landed work against ACs cannot confirm 4af8's deliverable is accepted; a follow-on phase could inadvertently reverse the entrypoint injection without any AC gate catching it.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC bullet 3 to: '…no re-export shims (S5); vendor mapper entrypoints (`InboundMapper`/`OutboundMapper`) injected into `outbound_differ`, `inbound_differ`, and `apply_inbound` via the port (`4af8`, change 914) — verified by contract-suite green on main.'
